---
title: "0.1 Setup your workspace"
author: "Minh-Quan Ton-Ngoc"
date: "2026-05-11"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-expand: true
    number-sections: true
    code-fold: true
    theme: cosmo
    self-contained: true
    smooth-scroll: true
    embed-resources: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
engine: knitr
---

```{=html}
<style>
.setup-note {
  background: #f8f9fa;
  border-left: 4px solid #0d6efd;
  padding: 1rem 1.2rem;
  border-radius: 0.4rem;
  margin-bottom: 1rem;
}
.check-card {
  background: #f6fff8;
  border-left: 4px solid #198754;
  padding: 1rem 1.2rem;
  border-radius: 0.4rem;
  margin: 1rem 0;
}
.manual-note {
  background: #fff8e1;
  border-left: 4px solid #f0ad4e;
  padding: 0.9rem 1rem;
  border-radius: 0.4rem;
  margin: 1rem 0;
}
.small-text {
  font-size: 0.95rem;
  color: #495057;
}
summary {
  font-weight: 600;
}
</style>
```

# Purpose

This guide helps you prepare a Windows or macOS laptop for the MicroGen 2026 workshop. It covers the tools used for coding, data analysis, and connecting to the workshop cloud server.

Most heavy bioinformatics workflows will run on the **workshop server**, not on your laptop. Your laptop mainly needs:

- a code editor,
- R and Python for small local exercises,
- SSH tools for connecting to the server,
- and optional graphical tools for users who prefer them.

::: {.callout-note}
## Required and optional tools

**Required for most participants:** Python 3.13, R 4.5.0, Visual Studio Code, the VS Code Remote - SSH extension, and the listed Python/R packages.

**Optional:** RStudio Desktop, Java, Artemis, Bandage, and extra X11/VNC tools. Instructors may ask you to use these for specific graphical activities.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## Do not share secrets

If you receive a `.pem` private key file, do not upload it, email it, paste it into chat, or share it with another participant. The key is your login credential for the server.
:::

# Quick checklist

| Tool | Required? | Used for | Windows 10/11 | macOS Intel | macOS Apple Silicon |
|---|---:|---|---|---|---|
| R 4.5.0 | Yes | R analysis notebooks and packages | CRAN Windows installer | CRAN `x86_64` macOS package | CRAN `arm64` macOS package |
| RStudio Desktop | Optional | Graphical R interface | Posit installer | Posit macOS app | Posit macOS app |
| Python 3.13 | Yes | Python exercises and checks | Python.org installer | Python.org installer or Homebrew | Python.org installer or Homebrew |
| Visual Studio Code | Yes | Editing files and remote server work | Windows installer | macOS Intel or Universal app | macOS Apple Silicon or Universal app |
| MobaXterm portable | Windows only | SSH, SFTP, X11, VNC tunnels | Recommended | Not used | Not used |
| Rtools45 | Sometimes | Build R packages from source if Windows package binaries are unavailable | Install only if needed | Not used | Not used |
| Java 11 or 17 | Optional | Mainly Artemis; sometimes other graphical tools | Temurin or other official JDK | Temurin or other official JDK | Temurin or other official JDK |
| Remote - SSH extension | Yes | Open server folders in VS Code | Install inside VS Code | Install inside VS Code | Install inside VS Code |
| X11/VNC tools | Optional | Remote graphical applications such as FastQC | MobaXterm; optionally VcXsrv or VNC viewer | XQuartz and Screen Sharing/VNC viewer | XQuartz and Screen Sharing/VNC viewer |

<details>
<summary><strong>Click to open official download links checked for this guide</strong></summary>

These links were checked against official project pages while preparing this guide:

- R and R 4.5.0: [CRAN](https://cran.r-project.org/), [Windows R 4.5.0 old release](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/old/4.5.0/), [macOS Intel R 4.5.0](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/big-sur-x86_64/base/), [macOS Apple Silicon R 4.5.0](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/big-sur-arm64/base/)
- RStudio Desktop: [Posit downloads](https://posit.co/downloads/)
- Python 3.13: [Python 3.13 latest maintenance release](https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/)
- Visual Studio Code: [VS Code download page](https://code.visualstudio.com/download)
- MobaXterm portable: [MobaXterm Home Edition](https://mobaxterm.mobatek.net/download-home-edition.html)
- Rtools for Windows: [CRAN Rtools](https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/)
- Java: [Eclipse Adoptium Temurin](https://adoptium.net/) or [Microsoft Build of OpenJDK](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/java/openjdk/download)
- Artemis: [Artemis official page](https://sanger-pathogens.github.io/Artemis/Artemis/) and [GitHub releases](https://github.com/sanger-pathogens/Artemis/releases)
- Bandage: [Bandage official page](https://rrwick.github.io/Bandage/) and [GitHub releases](https://github.com/rrwick/Bandage/releases)
- XQuartz: [XQuartz](https://www.xquartz.org/)

</details>

# Which instructions should I follow?

- If you use **Windows 10 or Windows 11**, follow [Windows 10/11 setup](#windows-1011-setup).
- If you use an **Intel Mac**, follow [macOS setup: Intel and Apple Silicon](#macos-setup-intel-and-apple-silicon), choosing the Intel options.
- If you use an **Apple Silicon Mac** with an M1, M2, M3, M4, or newer Apple chip, follow the Apple Silicon options.

::: {.callout-tip}
## How to check your Mac chip

Open the **Apple menu** in the top-left corner, then choose **About This Mac**.

- If you see **Processor: Intel**, use the Intel Mac instructions.
- If you see **Chip: Apple M1**, **M2**, **M3**, **M4**, or similar, use the Apple Silicon instructions.
:::

<img alt="Placeholder: macOS About This Mac screen showing Chip or Processor" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64," class="img-fluid" />

# Windows 10/11 setup

## Install Visual Studio Code

1. Open the official VS Code download page: <https://code.visualstudio.com/download>
2. Under **Windows**, download either:
   - **User Installer x64** if you are using your own laptop and do not have administrator rights, or
   - **System Installer x64** if your administrator or IT team prefers system-wide installation.
3. Run the installer.
4. When the installer shows optional tasks, tick these options if available:
   - **Add to PATH**
   - **Add "Open with Code" action**
   - **Register Code as an editor for supported file types**
5. Finish the installation and open VS Code.

In VS Code, open the Extensions view and install:

- **Remote - SSH** by Microsoft
- **Python** by Microsoft
- **R** by REditorSupport
- **Quarto** by Quarto

<img alt="Placeholder: VS Code Extensions panel with Remote - SSH, Python, R, and Quarto" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64," class="img-fluid" />

::: {.callout-note}
## What this does

VS Code is the main editor for the workshop. The Remote - SSH extension lets VS Code open your assigned server workspace as if it were a local folder.
:::

## Install Python 3.13

1. Open the official Python 3.13 download page: <https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/>
2. Download the **Windows installer (64-bit)**.
3. Run the installer.
4. On the first installer screen, tick **Add python.exe to PATH**.
5. Choose **Install Now**.

<img alt="Placeholder: Python installer screen showing Add python.exe to PATH" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64," class="img-fluid" />

::: {.callout-warning}
## Important Windows step

The **Add python.exe to PATH** checkbox is important. It lets Windows find Python when you type `python` in PowerShell.
:::

Open **PowerShell** from the Start menu and check Python:

```powershell
python --version
pip --version
```

You should see Python 3.13 and a `pip` version. `pip` is the tool Python uses to install Python packages.

Update `pip`:

```powershell
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
```

This asks Python to update its package installer.

Install the required Python packages:

```powershell
python -m pip install numpy pandas
```

This installs `numpy` and `pandas`, which are common Python packages for numerical and table-based data work.

Verify the packages:

```powershell
python -c "import numpy, pandas; print('Python packages OK')"
```

If the packages are installed correctly, PowerShell prints `Python packages OK`.

## Install R 4.5.0

R 4.5.0 is the R version used by the Day 4 practical materials. It is no longer the newest R release, so use the official CRAN old-release page for Windows.

1. Open the official CRAN Windows R 4.5.0 page: <https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/base/old/4.5.0/>
2. Download `R-4.5.0-win.exe`.
3. Run the installer.
4. Accept the default options unless your instructor tells you otherwise.

Open **R** or **RGui** from the Start menu and run:

```r
R.version.string
```

You should see a line beginning with `R version 4.5.0`.

## Install Rtools45 - only if needed

Most Windows users do **not** need Rtools at the start. R normally installs pre-built Windows package binaries from CRAN and Bioconductor.

Rtools is needed only when R says it must install a package **from source** or when an error mentions compilers, `make`, `gcc`, `g++`, `gfortran`, or `Rtools`.

For R 4.5.0 on Windows, use **Rtools45**.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Do not install a different Rtools version

Rtools must match your R version. For this workshop, Windows participants installing **R 4.5.0** should use **Rtools45**, not Rtools44, Rtools43, or older versions.
:::

1. Open the official CRAN Rtools page: <https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/>
2. Choose **RTools 4.5**.
3. Download the Rtools45 installer.
4. Run the installer.
5. Keep the default installation location, usually:

```text
C:\rtools45
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Beginner recommendation

If your R package installation works, you can skip Rtools. If installation fails with a compiler-related message, install Rtools45 and then retry the R package command.
:::

After installing Rtools45, open a fresh R or RStudio session and run:

```r
install.packages("pkgbuild")
pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)
```

If Rtools is detected, this should return `TRUE`.

## Install RStudio Desktop - optional

RStudio is optional. It is helpful if you prefer a graphical interface for R, but VS Code is enough for most workshop tasks.

1. Open the official Posit download page: <https://posit.co/downloads/>
2. Choose **RStudio Desktop**.
3. Download the Windows installer.
4. Run the installer with the default settings.
5. Open RStudio and check that it starts an R console.

::: {.callout-tip}
## RStudio cannot replace R

RStudio is an editor. R is the programming language. Install R first, then install RStudio.
:::

## Install R packages

Open **R**, **RGui**, or the **RStudio Console**. Paste this code and press Enter:

```r
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "dplyr",
  "tidyr",
  "readr",
  "ggplot2",
  "ggrepel",
  "vegan",
  "patchwork",
  "scales",
  "data.table",
  "broom",
  "knitr"
))
```

This installs the packages that come from CRAN.

Now install `BiocManager` if it is not already installed:

```r
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
```

`BiocManager` is the installer used for Bioconductor packages. Many learners will not have it yet.

Then install `phyloseq`:

```r
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
```

`phyloseq` comes from Bioconductor, so it is installed after `BiocManager` is available.

::: {.callout-note}
## If R asks about a CRAN mirror

Choose a mirror close to you, or choose the cloud mirror if it is listed. Any official CRAN mirror should work.
:::

Verify the R packages:

```r
packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "tidyr", "readr", "ggplot2", "ggrepel",
  "phyloseq", "vegan", "patchwork", "scales",
  "data.table", "broom", "knitr"
)

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
```

Each package should return `TRUE`. If one returns `FALSE`, copy the package name and ask an instructor for help.

::: {.callout-warning}
## If R asks to install from source on Windows

Choose the binary version if R offers one. If R says a package must be installed from source, you may need **Rtools45**. Install Rtools45 from the section above, restart R or RStudio, and run the package installation again.
:::

## Install MobaXterm portable

MobaXterm is recommended for Windows participants who need SSH, X11 forwarding, SFTP file browsing, or VNC tunneling. The portable edition does not require administrator installation.

1. Open the official MobaXterm Home Edition page: <https://mobaxterm.mobatek.net/download-home-edition.html>
2. Download the **Portable edition**.
3. Extract the ZIP file to a folder you can find later, such as `Downloads\MobaXterm`.
4. Open the extracted folder.
5. Run `MobaXterm_Personal_*.exe`.

<img alt="Placeholder: MobaXterm portable folder showing MobaXterm_Personal executable" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64," class="img-fluid" />

### Create an SSH session in MobaXterm

1. Open MobaXterm.
2. Click **Session**.
3. Choose **SSH**.
4. In **Remote host**, enter the server IP address given by the admin.
5. Tick **Specify username** and enter your username.
6. Open **Advanced SSH settings**.
7. Tick **Use private key** and select your `.pem` file.
8. Make sure **X11-forwarding** is enabled.
9. Click **OK** to connect.
10. Save the session if MobaXterm asks.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Keep the key file path simple

If possible, store your `.pem` key in a folder path without spaces, for example `C:\Users\YourName\Downloads\microgen2026.pem`. If the path contains spaces, keep quotation marks around it when using PowerShell.
:::

## Install Java - optional

Java is optional for most participants. It is mainly needed for **Artemis**. Bandage is usually provided as a standalone app and does not normally require Java.

Use a stable official JDK distribution, such as:

- Eclipse Temurin: <https://adoptium.net/>
- Microsoft Build of OpenJDK: <https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/java/openjdk/download>

For the workshop, Java 17 is a good default. Java 11 is also usually compatible with recent Artemis releases.

After installation, open PowerShell:

```powershell
java -version
```

You should see a Java version number.

<details>
<summary><strong>Click to open optional Artemis and Bandage download notes</strong></summary>

Artemis official page: <https://sanger-pathogens.github.io/Artemis/Artemis/>

Artemis GitHub releases: <https://github.com/sanger-pathogens/Artemis/releases>

Bandage official page: <https://rrwick.github.io/Bandage/>

Bandage GitHub releases: <https://github.com/rrwick/Bandage/releases>

For Artemis on Windows, download the Windows release ZIP, extract it, and open `artemis.jar` after Java is installed.

For Bandage on Windows, download the Windows release ZIP, extract it, and run the Bandage executable. No installation is usually needed.

</details>

# macOS setup: Intel and Apple Silicon

## Check chip type

1. Open the **Apple menu** in the top-left corner.
2. Choose **About This Mac**.
3. Look for **Chip** or **Processor**.

Use this rule:

- **Processor: Intel** means Intel Mac.
- **Chip: Apple M1/M2/M3/M4** means Apple Silicon Mac.

## Install Visual Studio Code

1. Open the official VS Code download page: <https://code.visualstudio.com/download>
2. Under **Mac**, choose:
   - **Intel chip** for Intel Macs,
   - **Apple silicon** for M1/M2/M3/M4 Macs,
   - or **Universal** if you are unsure.
3. Open the downloaded `.dmg` file.
4. Drag **Visual Studio Code** to the **Applications** folder.
5. Open VS Code from Applications.

Install these VS Code extensions:

- **Remote - SSH** by Microsoft
- **Python** by Microsoft
- **R** by REditorSupport
- **Quarto** by Quarto

## Install Python 3.13

There are two acceptable routes. Use Route A if you are not comfortable with Terminal.

### Route A: official Python installer

1. Open the official Python 3.13 download page: <https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/>
2. Download the **macOS installer**.
3. Open the `.pkg` installer.
4. Follow the default installation steps.

Open **Terminal** from Applications > Utilities and check Python:

```bash
python3 --version
python3 -m pip --version
```

### Route B: Homebrew - optional

Use this only if you already use Homebrew or are comfortable with Terminal.

Install Python 3.13:

```bash
brew install python@3.13
```

This asks Homebrew to install Python 3.13.

Check Python:

```bash
python3 --version
python3 -m pip --version
```

Install Python packages:

```bash
python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip
python3 -m pip install numpy pandas
```

The first command updates the Python package installer. The second installs `numpy` and `pandas`.

Verify:

```bash
python3 -c "import numpy, pandas; print('Python packages OK')"
```

## Install R 4.5.0

R 4.5.0 is now an older R release. CRAN still provides the official installers in architecture-specific macOS directories.

### Intel Mac

1. Open the CRAN Intel macOS base directory: <https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/big-sur-x86_64/base/>
2. Download `R-4.5.0-x86_64.pkg`.
3. Open the `.pkg` file and accept the default installation options.

### Apple Silicon Mac

1. Open the CRAN Apple Silicon macOS base directory: <https://cran.r-project.org/bin/macosx/big-sur-arm64/base/>
2. Download `R-4.5.0-arm64.pkg`.
3. Open the `.pkg` file and accept the default installation options.

::: {.callout-note}
## macOS version note

The R 4.5.0 macOS packages were built for modern macOS versions. If your Mac is older or cannot install the package, ask an instructor before installing a different R version.
:::

Verify in Terminal:

```bash
R --version
```

Or open **R.app** and run:

```r
R.version.string
```

You should see R 4.5.0.

## Install RStudio Desktop - optional

1. Open the official Posit download page: <https://posit.co/downloads/>
2. Download **RStudio Desktop** for macOS.
3. Open the downloaded file.
4. Drag RStudio to **Applications** if prompted.
5. Open RStudio and confirm that it detects R.

## Install R packages

Open **R.app** or **RStudio Console**. Paste:

```r
install.packages(c(
  "tidyverse",
  "dplyr",
  "tidyr",
  "readr",
  "ggplot2",
  "ggrepel",
  "vegan",
  "patchwork",
  "scales",
  "data.table",
  "broom",
  "knitr"
))
```

This installs the packages that come from CRAN.

Now install `BiocManager` if it is not already installed:

```r
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
```

`BiocManager` is the installer used for Bioconductor packages. Many learners will not have it yet.

Then install `phyloseq`:

```r
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
```

`phyloseq` comes from Bioconductor, so it is installed after `BiocManager` is available.

Verify:

```r
packages <- c(
  "dplyr", "tidyr", "readr", "ggplot2", "ggrepel",
  "phyloseq", "vegan", "patchwork", "scales",
  "data.table", "broom", "knitr"
)

sapply(packages, requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
```

If R asks whether to install from source, choose the **binary** option if it is available.

::: {.callout-note}
## Rtools is not used on macOS

Rtools is for Windows only. On macOS, packages that need compilation use Apple's Command Line Tools instead.
:::

If compilation fails on macOS, install the Xcode Command Line Tools:

```bash
xcode-select --install
```

This opens an Apple installer for command-line build tools. After it finishes, reopen R or RStudio and try the package installation again.

## Install Java - optional

Java is optional. It is mainly needed for Artemis.

Use a stable official JDK distribution:

- Eclipse Temurin: <https://adoptium.net/>
- Microsoft Build of OpenJDK: <https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/java/openjdk/download>

Choose the correct installer:

- Intel Mac: macOS `x64`
- Apple Silicon Mac: macOS `aarch64` or `Apple Silicon`

Verify in Terminal:

```bash
java -version
```

<details>
<summary><strong>Click to open optional Artemis and Bandage notes for macOS</strong></summary>

Artemis is a Java application. Recent Artemis releases require Java 9 or newer, and Java 11 or 17 is a sensible workshop choice.

Download Artemis from the official page:

<https://sanger-pathogens.github.io/Artemis/Artemis/>

Bandage is not a Java application. Use the official Bandage release page and choose the Mac build for your chip:

<https://github.com/rrwick/Bandage/releases>

On macOS, if you see a warning that an app is from an unidentified developer, right-click the app and choose **Open**. If the instructor asks you to clear a quarantine flag for Bandage, use:

```bash
xattr -cr /Applications/Bandage.app
```

</details>

# Advanced: R in VS Code

This section is optional. It is for participants who already feel comfortable with VS Code and want a richer R editing environment than the basic workshop setup.

Use this only after you have already installed:

- R 4.5.0,
- Visual Studio Code,
- and the **R** extension by **REditorSupport**.

Official VS Code reference:

<https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/r>

::: {.callout-note}
## Basic users can skip this

You do not need this advanced setup to complete the workshop. The basic R installation and the VS Code R extension are enough for most learners.
:::

## Who should use this?

Use this setup if you are comfortable editing VS Code settings and want:

- R code completion and IntelliSense,
- a better plot viewer inside VS Code,
- R workspace, data, help, and plot viewers,
- linting warnings while editing R code,
- shortcuts for common R operators,
- and optional debugging tools.

If you are new to R or VS Code, skip this section for now. You can return to it later.

## What the advanced setup adds

The VS Code R extension can provide:

- R syntax highlighting and code completion,
- R terminals inside VS Code,
- linting and formatting support,
- plot, data, help, and workspace viewers,
- R Markdown support,
- and optional debugging through a separate R Debugger extension.

## Step 1: Install R packages used by VS Code

Open **R**, **RGui**, or the **RStudio Console**. Paste this code and press Enter:

```r
if (!require("languageserver")) {
  install.packages("languageserver", repos = "https://packagemanager.posit.co/cran/latest")
}

if (!require("rmarkdown")) {
  install.packages("rmarkdown", repos = "https://packagemanager.posit.co/cran/latest")
}

if (!require("httpgd")) {
  install.packages("httpgd", repos = "https://packagemanager.posit.co/cran/latest")
}
```

What they do:

- `languageserver` enables R IntelliSense, code completion, function help, go-to-definition, and related language features.
- `rmarkdown` supports `.Rmd` documents.
- `httpgd` enables the interactive plot viewer used by the VS Code R extension.

::: {.callout-note}
## Personal R library prompt

If R asks to create a personal library folder in your user directory, accept it. This usually happens when the system-wide R library is not writable without administrator permissions.
:::

## Step 2: Install the correct VS Code extension

In VS Code:

1. Open **Extensions**.
2. Search for `R`.
3. Install **R** by **REditorSupport**.

Avoid installing several R extensions that do the same job. Multiple overlapping R extensions can cause duplicate commands, conflicting terminals, or confusing error messages.

## Step 3: Create and use an R terminal

The official VS Code workflow is to send R code to an R terminal.

Open the Command Palette:

- Windows: `Ctrl+Shift+P`
- macOS: `Command+Shift+P`

Run:

```text
R: Create R terminal
```

Then open an `.R` file and press:

```text
Ctrl+Enter
```

This sends the current line or selected R code to the active R terminal.

To source an entire R file, use:

```text
Ctrl+Shift+S
```

## Step 4: Add recommended VS Code settings

Open VS Code settings JSON:

1. Open the Command Palette.
2. Run:

```text
Preferences: Open User Settings (JSON)
```

3. Add these settings, merging them with any settings already present:

<details>
<summary><strong>Click to open suggested VS Code settings for advanced R users</strong></summary>

```json
{
  "editor.formatOnType": true,
  "editor.minimap.enabled": false,
  "r.plot.useHttpgd": true,
  "editor.suggest.selectionMode": "whenQuickSuggestion",
  "r.session.useWebServer": true,
  "r.session.data.rowLimit": 50000,
  "r.session.data.pageSize": 250
}
```

These settings enable `httpgd`, R session viewer features, and a few useful editor preferences.

Do not replace your whole settings file if it already contains other settings. Merge only the entries you need, and keep valid JSON syntax.

</details>

## Step 5: Optional keybindings

These shortcuts are optional. Add them only if you want familiar R operator shortcuts in VS Code.

Open keyboard shortcuts JSON:

1. Open the Command Palette.
2. Run:

```text
Preferences: Open Keyboard Shortcuts (JSON)
```

3. Add the bindings below.

Useful shortcuts included:

| Shortcut | Inserts |
|---|---|
| `Alt+-` | `<-` |
| `Ctrl+Shift+M` | `%>%` |
| `Ctrl+Shift+C` | Toggle line comment |
| `Ctrl+Shift+F10` | Kill terminal |

<details>
<summary><strong>Click to open optional R keybindings</strong></summary>

```json
[
  {
    "key": "ctrl+shift+c",
    "command": "editor.action.commentLine",
    "when": "editorTextFocus && !editorReadonly"
  },
  {
    "key": "ctrl+/",
    "command": "-editor.action.commentLine",
    "when": "editorTextFocus && !editorReadonly"
  },
  {
    "key": "Ctrl+Shift+m",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " %>% " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == r"
  },
  {
    "key": "Alt+-",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " <- " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == r"
  },
  {
    "key": "Ctrl+Shift+m",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " %>% " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == rmd"
  },
  {
    "key": "Alt+-",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " <- " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == rmd"
  },
  {
    "key": "Ctrl+Shift+m",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " %>% " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == quarto"
  },
  {
    "key": "Alt+-",
    "command": "type",
    "args": { "text": " <- " },
    "when": "editorTextFocus && editorLangId == quarto"
  },
  {
    "key": "Ctrl+Shift+m",
    "command": "workbench.action.terminal.sendSequence",
    "args": { "text": " %>% " },
    "when": "terminalFocus"
  },
  {
    "key": "Alt+-",
    "command": "workbench.action.terminal.sendSequence",
    "args": { "text": " <- " },
    "when": "terminalFocus"
  },
  {
    "key": "ctrl+shift+f10",
    "command": "workbench.action.terminal.kill"
  }
]
```

</details>

If these shortcuts conflict with shortcuts you already use, remove or edit the conflicting entries. The keybindings are not required for the workshop.

## Step 6: Optional linting configuration

The VS Code R extension can use the R package `lintr` for linting. Linting means showing style and code-quality warnings while you edit.

Install `lintr` in R:

```r
install.packages("lintr")
```

To reduce distracting style warnings during workshop-style code, create a file named `.lintr` in your user home folder or project root.

Example Windows location:

```text
C:\Users\YourName\.lintr
```

Example macOS location:

```text
/Users/YourName/.lintr
```

Paste this content into `.lintr`:

```text
linters: linters_with_defaults(
  trailing_blank_lines_linter = NULL,
  line_length_linter = NULL,
  trailing_whitespace_linter = NULL,
  commented_code_linter = NULL,
  indentation_linter = NULL,
  object_usage_linter = NULL,
  object_name_linter = NULL
  )
```

This turns off several warnings that are often distracting during short practical exercises.

## Optional radian console

The official VS Code R documentation recommends `radian` as an optional modern R console. It provides syntax highlighting and a better console experience than the default R terminal.

Install it only if you are comfortable with Python package installation.

Windows:

```powershell
python -m pip install radian
```

macOS:

```bash
python3 -m pip install radian
```

After installing, follow the R extension documentation if you want VS Code to use `radian` as the R terminal. Most workshop exercises do not require `radian`.

## Optional R debugging

R debugging in VS Code is provided by the separate **R Debugger** extension.

Install it only if you need breakpoints and step-through debugging:

<https://marketplace.visualstudio.com/items?itemName=RDebugger.r-debugger>

Most learners do not need R debugging for the workshop.

## Quick verification

Create a file called `test-r-vscode.R`:

```r
x <- rnorm(100)
summary(x)
plot(x)
```

In VS Code:

1. Run `R: Create R terminal`.
2. Open `test-r-vscode.R`.
3. Press `Ctrl+Enter` on each line.

Expected result:

- R code runs in the R terminal.
- Completion appears while typing R functions.
- The plot appears in the VS Code plot viewer if `httpgd` is installed and enabled.

## Advanced R in VS Code troubleshooting

### VS Code does not find R

On Windows, reinstall R and make sure the installer saves the R version in the registry if that option appears. This helps the R extension find the R executable automatically.

You can also start R manually in a normal VS Code terminal if the automatic `R: Create R terminal` command fails.

### `languageserver` fails to install

Likely cause: R needs to compile dependencies.

On Windows, install **Rtools45** if the error mentions compilers or source packages. On macOS, install Xcode Command Line Tools:

```bash
xcode-select --install
```

Then restart R and try again.

### Plot viewer does not work

Check that `httpgd` is installed:

```r
requireNamespace("httpgd", quietly = TRUE)
```

Check that VS Code settings include:

```json
"r.plot.useHttpgd": true
```

Restart VS Code after changing settings.

### Too many lint warnings appear

Use the `.lintr` configuration above, or temporarily disable linting in VS Code settings.

### Keyboard shortcuts conflict with existing shortcuts

Remove or edit the conflicting entries in your keyboard shortcuts JSON. The shortcuts are optional.

# Server access setup

This section is adapted from the MicroGen 2026 instructor access guide. Your exact server IP address, username, and key file will be provided by the workshop admin.

## What information you will receive from the admin

You should receive:

- your username,
- the server IP address,
- a `.pem` private key file,
- and, if relevant, your assigned instance name.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Private key safety

Do not share the `.pem` key. Do not paste it into this notebook, a chat window, email, or a shared document.
:::

## SSH from Windows PowerShell

Open PowerShell and replace `username`, `SERVER_IP`, and the key path with your real details:

```powershell
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i "C:\path\to\key.pem"
```

The quotation marks are important if the path contains spaces.

After login, check your server location:

```bash
whoami
pwd
ls -lh /workspace/$USER /scratch/$USER
```

Use `/workspace/<username>` for exercises and outputs you want to keep. Use `/scratch/<username>` for large temporary files.

## SSH from macOS Terminal

Before your first login, fix the key file permissions:

```bash
chmod 600 /path/to/key.pem
```

This tells macOS that only you can read the key file.

Connect:

```bash
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
```

After login:

```bash
whoami
pwd
ls -lh /workspace/$USER /scratch/$USER
```

## VS Code Remote SSH

<img alt="Placeholder: VS Code Remote SSH connection from laptop to server" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64," class="img-fluid" />

1. Open VS Code.
2. Install the **Remote - SSH** extension.
3. Open the Command Palette:
   - Windows: `Ctrl+Shift+P`
   - macOS: `Command+Shift+P`
4. Choose **Remote-SSH: Add New SSH Host**.
5. Paste your SSH command, for example:

```text
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
```

6. Choose the SSH configuration file suggested by VS Code.
7. Open the Command Palette again.
8. Choose **Remote-SSH: Connect to Host**.
9. Select your new host.
10. Open your assigned workspace folder:

```text
/workspace/<username>
```

Do not use `/databases` as your main VS Code workspace. Shared database files are usually read-only.

### Example SSH config

Windows example:

```text
Host microgen2026
    HostName SERVER_IP
    User username
    IdentityFile C:\path\to\key.pem
```

macOS example:

```text
Host microgen2026
    HostName SERVER_IP
    User username
    IdentityFile /path/to/key.pem
```

::: {.callout-tip}
## Long-running commands

If you run a long analysis on the server, use `tmux` so the command can continue if your network drops:

```bash
tmux new -s microgen
```

Detach with `Ctrl-b`, then press `d`. Return later with:

```bash
tmux attach -t microgen
```
:::

## X11 forwarding

X11 opens a single graphical program from the server on your laptop. For example, the `fastqc` window can appear on your own screen while the program runs on the server.

### Windows: MobaXterm recommended

MobaXterm includes an X server.

1. Open MobaXterm.
2. Check that the X server is running.
3. Create or open your SSH session.
4. Make sure X11 forwarding is enabled in the SSH session settings.
5. In the server terminal, run:

```bash
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
```

If a clock window opens, X11 is working. You can then try:

```bash
fastqc
```

### Windows: VcXsrv alternative

If you use VcXsrv instead of MobaXterm:

1. Install VcXsrv.
2. Open **XLaunch**.
3. Choose:

```text
Multiple windows
Start no client
Clipboard enabled
Disable access control
```

PowerShell uses this syntax:

```powershell
$env:DISPLAY = "127.0.0.1:0.0"
echo $env:DISPLAY
ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i "C:\path\to\key.pem"
```

Inside the SSH session:

```bash
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
fastqc
```

::: {.callout-warning}
## PowerShell is not CMD

Do not use `set DISPLAY=...` in PowerShell. Use `$env:DISPLAY = "127.0.0.1:0.0"`.
:::

### macOS: XQuartz

macOS requires XQuartz for X11.

1. Install XQuartz from <https://www.xquartz.org/>.
2. Open XQuartz once.
3. Close and reopen Terminal.
4. Connect with trusted X11 forwarding:

```bash
ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
```

Test:

```bash
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
fastqc
```

If the window does not appear, make sure XQuartz is running. If X11 is slow, use VNC instead.

## VNC access

VNC opens a full remote desktop. It is often more stable than X11 for graphical tools.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Use VNC only through SSH tunnel

Do not connect directly to a public VNC port. Start VNC on the server, create an SSH tunnel, then connect your VNC client to `localhost`.
:::

<img alt="Placeholder: VNC through SSH tunnel concept" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64," class="img-fluid" />

### Start VNC on the server

First, SSH to the server. Then run:

```bash
start-vnc
```

The first time, it asks you to create a VNC password.

Important:

- this is a separate VNC password,
- it is not your Linux password,
- it is not your private key,
- when asked for a view-only password, type `n`.

The command prints something like:

```text
Display: :2
VNC port: 5902
```

Write down the `VNC port`. The port may be `5901`, `5902`, `5903`, or another number.

### Generic SSH tunnel

Open a **new** terminal window on your laptop and keep your original SSH session open. Replace `5902` with the port printed by `start-vnc`:

```bash
ssh -L 5902:localhost:5902 username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
```

Keep this tunnel window open.

Then connect your VNC client to:

```text
localhost:5902
```

If your VNC port is different, use that number instead.

### Windows VNC with MobaXterm

In MobaXterm:

1. Start VNC on the server with `start-vnc`.
2. Open **Tunneling > New SSH tunnel**.
3. Choose local port forwarding.
4. Fill in:

```text
Forwarded port: your VNC port
Remote server: localhost
Remote port: your VNC port
SSH server: SERVER_IP
SSH login: username
SSH port: 22
Use private key: key.pem
```

Example if `start-vnc` reports port `5902`:

```text
Forwarded port: 5902
Remote server: localhost
Remote port: 5902
SSH server: SERVER_IP
SSH login: username
SSH port: 22
Use private key: key.pem
```

Start the tunnel and keep it running.

Then create a VNC session:

```text
Remote hostname: localhost
Port: your VNC port
```

Enter the VNC password you created with `start-vnc`.

### macOS VNC

macOS includes Screen Sharing.

1. Start VNC on the server with `start-vnc`.
2. Open a new Terminal window and create the SSH tunnel:

```bash
ssh -L 5902:localhost:5902 username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
```

3. In Finder, choose:

```text
Go > Connect to Server
```

4. Enter:

```text
vnc://localhost:5902
```

5. Enter the VNC password you created with `start-vnc`.

You can also use RealVNC Viewer or TigerVNC Viewer if Screen Sharing does not work well on your Mac.

### Stop VNC when finished

List your VNC sessions:

```bash
list-vnc
```

If the display is `:2`, stop it with:

```bash
stop-vnc 2
```

If the display is `:1`, stop it with:

```bash
stop-vnc 1
```

If the remote desktop is locked, run:

```bash
fix-vnc-lock
list-vnc
```

Then restart your own VNC display.

# Verification checklist

Run the checks for your operating system. It is fine if `java -version` fails when you have not installed Java.

## Windows PowerShell

```powershell
python --version
pip --version
java -version
ssh -V
```

## R console

```r
R.version.string
sapply(c("dplyr", "ggplot2", "phyloseq", "vegan"), requireNamespace, quietly = TRUE)
```

## macOS Terminal

```bash
python3 --version
R --version
java -version
ssh -V
```

<div class="check-card">
If Python, R, SSH, and the R package check work, your laptop is ready for the core workshop activities.
</div>

# Troubleshooting

## `python` is not recognized on Windows

Likely cause: Python was installed without adding it to PATH.

Simple fix:

1. Open the Python installer again.
2. Choose **Modify** or reinstall Python.
3. Tick **Add python.exe to PATH** if shown.
4. Close and reopen PowerShell.
5. Try:

```powershell
python --version
```

Ask an instructor if Windows still cannot find Python.

## `pip` is not recognized

Likely cause: Windows can find Python but not the separate `pip` command.

Simple fix: use `python -m pip` instead:

```powershell
python -m pip --version
python -m pip install numpy pandas
```

Ask for help if `python -m pip` also fails.

## R package installation fails

Likely causes:

- internet connection problem,
- CRAN mirror problem,
- missing system build tools,
- `BiocManager` has not been installed yet for Bioconductor packages,
- or an older package binary is not available.

Simple fixes:

1. Try again with a different CRAN mirror.
2. Restart R or RStudio.
3. If the error mentions `BiocManager`, install it first:

```r
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}
```

4. On Windows, if the error mentions compilers, `make`, `gcc`, `g++`, `gfortran`, or `Rtools`, install Rtools45 from the official CRAN Rtools page:

```text
https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
```

5. On macOS, install Xcode Command Line Tools:

```bash
xcode-select --install
```

Ask an instructor if the error continues after these steps, especially if it mentions Fortran, `gfortran`, unavailable binary packages, or incompatible Bioconductor versions.

## `phyloseq` installation fails

Likely cause: `phyloseq` comes from Bioconductor, not ordinary CRAN, so it needs `BiocManager`.

Simple fix:

```r
if (!requireNamespace("BiocManager", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("BiocManager")
}

BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
```

Ask an instructor if Bioconductor says your R version is not compatible.

## Windows says Rtools is required

Likely cause: R needs to build one or more packages from source.

Simple fix:

1. Install **Rtools45** from the official CRAN page:

```text
https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
```

2. Keep the default installation folder:

```text
C:\rtools45
```

3. Restart R or RStudio.
4. Check that R can find Rtools:

```r
install.packages("pkgbuild")
pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)
```

5. Retry the failed package installation.

Ask an instructor for help if `pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)` does not return `TRUE`.

## macOS asks for command line tools

Likely cause: an R package needs to compile code.

Simple fix:

```bash
xcode-select --install
```

Wait for the installer to finish, then reopen R or RStudio.

## VS Code cannot connect to the server

Likely causes:

- wrong server IP,
- wrong username,
- wrong key path,
- key file was moved,
- server not ready,
- or local network blocks SSH.

Simple checks:

1. Try the same SSH command in PowerShell or Terminal.
2. Check that the key file exists at the path in your SSH config.
3. Check that `HostName`, `User`, and `IdentityFile` are correct.

Ask the admin if ordinary SSH fails too.

## SSH says `Permission denied`

Likely causes:

- wrong username,
- wrong private key,
- private key not selected,
- or the admin has not added your account yet.

Simple fix: check the command carefully:

```bash
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
```

Ask the admin if the username, server IP, and key are correct but the error continues.

## SSH says `Bad permissions`

This is common on macOS when the key file is readable by other users.

Fix:

```bash
chmod 600 /path/to/key.pem
```

Then connect again.

## PEM key path contains spaces

On Windows, put quotation marks around the key path:

```powershell
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i "C:\Users\Your Name\Downloads\microgen2026.pem"
```

On macOS, either quote the path or move the key to a simpler location:

```bash
ssh username@SERVER_IP -i "/Users/Your Name/Downloads/microgen2026.pem"
```

## MobaXterm cannot open GUI windows

Likely causes:

- the MobaXterm X server is not running,
- X11 forwarding is not enabled,
- or the remote program cannot find `DISPLAY`.

Simple checks inside the server SSH session:

```bash
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
```

If `xclock` does not open, use VNC or ask an instructor to check the MobaXterm session settings.

## X11 forwarding does not work

Likely causes:

- XQuartz is not running on macOS,
- VcXsrv is not running on Windows,
- the SSH session was opened without `-Y`,
- or network latency makes X11 unstable.

Simple fix on macOS:

```bash
ssh -Y username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
```

If it remains unreliable, use VNC.

## VNC tunnel connects but the screen is blank

Likely causes:

- you connected to the wrong port,
- the tunnel window was closed,
- VNC was not started on the server,
- or the remote desktop is locked.

Simple checks on the server:

```bash
list-vnc
fix-vnc-lock
```

If needed, stop your display and start again:

```bash
stop-vnc 2
start-vnc
```

Replace `2` with your actual display number.

## Java is not found

Likely cause: Java was not installed or the terminal has not refreshed its PATH.

Simple fixes:

1. Install Java 17 from an official JDK provider.
2. Close and reopen PowerShell or Terminal.
3. Run:

```bash
java -version
```

Ask for help if Java is installed but still not found.

## RStudio cannot find R

Likely cause: RStudio was installed before R, or R installation did not finish.

Simple fixes:

1. Install R 4.5.0.
2. Restart the computer.
3. Open RStudio again.

If RStudio still cannot find R, ask an instructor to check the R installation path.

# FAQs

This section answers common setup questions in the way they often appear during installation.

## Do I need to install Rtools45 before class?

Usually no. Install Rtools45 only if you are on Windows and R reports that it needs to build a package from source.

You probably need Rtools45 if you see errors mentioning:

- `Rtools`
- `make`
- `gcc`
- `g++`
- `gfortran`
- `compilation failed`
- `package ... is only available in source form`

If you use Windows with R 4.5.0, install **Rtools45** from:

```text
https://cran.r-project.org/bin/windows/Rtools/
```

Keep the default location:

```text
C:\rtools45
```

Then restart R or RStudio and retry the package installation.

## I installed Rtools45. Why does R still not find it?

Likely cause: R or RStudio was already open before Rtools45 was installed.

Simple fix:

1. Close all R, RGui, and RStudio windows.
2. Reopen R or RStudio.
3. Run:

```r
install.packages("pkgbuild")
pkgbuild::has_rtools(debug = TRUE)
```

If it still does not return `TRUE`, check that Rtools45 was installed in `C:\rtools45`. Ask an instructor if your institution's laptop security policy blocks the installer.

## Should macOS users install Rtools?

No. Rtools is for Windows only.

On macOS, if R needs to compile a package, install Apple's Command Line Tools:

```bash
xcode-select --install
```

After the installer finishes, close and reopen R or RStudio.

## R says `there is no package called 'BiocManager'`. What should I do?

Install `BiocManager` first:

```r
install.packages("BiocManager")
```

Then install the Bioconductor package:

```r
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
```

`BiocManager` is not included with a fresh R installation, so this message is normal on a new computer.

## R asks `Do you want to install from sources the package which needs compilation?`

For beginners, choose **No** if R also offers a binary package. Binary packages are easier to install.

If R cannot offer a binary package and says source installation is required:

- Windows: install Rtools45, restart R or RStudio, and try again.
- macOS: install Xcode Command Line Tools, restart R or RStudio, and try again.

Ask an instructor if the package is still failing after the build tools are installed.

## R asks me to update many packages. Should I say yes?

Usually yes, but it can take time. If you are in the workshop room and the internet is slow, ask an instructor first.

For the `phyloseq` command in this guide, we use:

```r
BiocManager::install("phyloseq", ask = FALSE, update = TRUE)
```

This allows Bioconductor to update packages needed for compatibility.

## I see `package is not available for this version of R`.

Likely causes:

- you installed the wrong R version,
- the package repository was temporarily unavailable,
- or R is using a stale package mirror.

Simple checks:

```r
R.version.string
```

You should see R 4.5.0 for this workshop. If the version is different, ask an instructor whether to reinstall R 4.5.0 or continue with your current version.

## Python installed, but `python --version` does not work on Windows.

Likely cause: Python was installed without adding it to PATH.

Simple fix:

1. Re-run the Python installer.
2. Choose **Modify** or reinstall.
3. Tick **Add python.exe to PATH**.
4. Close and reopen PowerShell.
5. Run:

```powershell
python --version
```

If that still fails, try:

```powershell
py --version
```

## `pip install numpy pandas` fails.

Use Python's module form of `pip`, which is more reliable:

```powershell
python -m pip install --upgrade pip
python -m pip install numpy pandas
```

On macOS:

```bash
python3 -m pip install --upgrade pip
python3 -m pip install numpy pandas
```

If the error mentions internet access, try a different network or ask an instructor.

## VS Code Remote SSH asks me to choose Linux, Windows, or macOS for the server.

Choose **Linux**. The workshop server is a Linux server.

## VS Code Remote SSH keeps asking for a password.

Likely causes:

- VS Code cannot find your `.pem` key file,
- the key path in the SSH config is wrong,
- or the server username is wrong.

Open your SSH config and check:

```text
Host microgen2026
    HostName SERVER_IP
    User username
    IdentityFile /path/to/key.pem
```

On Windows, a key path may look like:

```text
IdentityFile C:\Users\YourName\Downloads\microgen2026.pem
```

Ask the admin if the username, server IP, and key path are all correct but login still fails.

## SSH says `WARNING: UNPROTECTED PRIVATE KEY FILE`.

This usually happens on macOS or Linux when the `.pem` file permissions are too open.

Run:

```bash
chmod 600 /path/to/key.pem
```

Then try SSH again.

## MobaXterm opens SSH, but graphical windows do not appear.

Check X11 first:

```bash
echo $DISPLAY
xclock
```

If `xclock` does not appear, check that MobaXterm's X server is running and that X11 forwarding is enabled in the session settings.

If X11 remains slow or unreliable, use VNC instead.

## VNC asks for a password. Is that my server password?

No. It is the VNC password you created when you ran:

```bash
start-vnc
```

It is separate from your `.pem` key and separate from any Linux password.

If you forgot the VNC password, ask an instructor before resetting it.

## The VNC port is not `5901`. Is that a problem?

No. Use the port printed by `start-vnc`, for example `5902` or `5903`.

If `start-vnc` prints:

```text
VNC port: 5902
```

then your tunnel should use `5902`:

```bash
ssh -L 5902:localhost:5902 username@SERVER_IP -i /path/to/key.pem
```

## Java is optional. Should I install it now?

Install Java only if your instructor asks you to use Artemis or another Java-based graphical tool.

For this workshop, Java 17 is a good default. After installing, check:

```bash
java -version
```

If you are short on time before class, prioritize R, Python, VS Code, and server access first.

# Further reading before the workshop

Official beginner-friendly references:

- VS Code Remote SSH: <https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/remote/ssh>
- VS Code R language guide: <https://code.visualstudio.com/docs/languages/r>
- VS Code download page: <https://code.visualstudio.com/download>
- Python downloads: <https://www.python.org/downloads/>
- Python 3.13 latest release: <https://www.python.org/downloads/latest/python3.13/>
- CRAN R project: <https://cran.r-project.org/>
- Posit RStudio Desktop: <https://posit.co/downloads/>
- Quarto getting started: <https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/>
- MobaXterm documentation and downloads: <https://mobaxterm.mobatek.net/>
- XQuartz: <https://www.xquartz.org/>
- Nextflow basic concepts: <https://www.nextflow.io/docs/latest/basic.html>
- Artemis: <https://sanger-pathogens.github.io/Artemis/Artemis/>
- Bandage: <https://rrwick.github.io/Bandage/>
- MicroGen 2026 Day 4 instructions: see the workshop website and the Day 4 lesson index.
